evidence: Docstring: n distinct points in R^2 forming a convex polygon have a vertex with at least floor(n/2) distinct distances to other vertices.
Docstring asks whether for every prime p there is a prime q <= p which is a primitive root modulo p.
The statement quantifies over primes p and asks for a prime q that is a primitive root modulo p.
Docstring: Heath-Brown proved that at least one of 2, 3, or 5 is a primitive root for infinitely many primes p.
Heath-Brown's 2/3/5 result is the classical partial result toward Artin's conjecture, which the file's TODO note names explicitly.
A_k is defined as the limsup of |sum_j e(k x_j)| with e(x)=e^{2 pi i x}, i.e. the growth of exponential sums along the sequence.
Docstring: the A_k <= k+1 bound is witnessed via the (shifted) van der Corput sequence.
Docstring asks whether every diameter-minimizing n-point set with minimum distance 1 must contain an equilateral triangle of side length 1.
The hypothesis bounds the L^2 error of the Fourier partial sums f_k (fourierPartial via fourierCoeff) of f on the circle.
The conclusion is a strong-law averaging statement (1/N) sum f(n_k x) -> integral of f; both references [Er49d] and [Ma66] are titled 'On the strong law of large numbers'.
The problem asks whether {alpha p_n} is never well-distributed, the Hlawka-Petersen strengthening of equidistribution defined by IsWellDistributed.
The sequence in question is {alpha p_n} where p_n is the sequence of primes (Nat.nth Nat.Prime).
Docstring asks whether every set of n positive integers contains a sum-free subset of size at least n/3 + Omega(n).
The statement counts tuples with x_i + y_j in A for a dense subset A of a finite abelian group G.
The problem asks whether the 2-colour van der Waerden number W(k, r) is polynomial in r for fixed k.
W(k, r) is defined via colourings forced to contain a monochromatic arithmetic progression of length k or r.
